title: TBM 407: The Three Juggling Acts (Strategic, Lazy, and Survival)
questions: Where are we actually operating right now (strategic, lazy, or survival), and what evidence would prove it?
Which commitments are true optional bets versus fear-driven balls we refuse to drop?
If we had to cut half of what we’re juggling tomorrow, what would we keep—and what would that reveal?
What would it take to create breathing room to shift even temporarily toward strategic modes?
search_terms: portfolio of bets drift into crisis
summary: The post proposes a three-mode model for how teams manage work under uncertainty: strategic juggling (intentional optionality), lazy juggling (novelty-driven drift without learning), and survival juggling (overload with forced tradeoffs). It is aimed at leaders and operators who want to diagnose whether their “portfolio of bets” is real disciplined strategy or a drift toward crisis, and how to recover agency by stabilizing, creating breathing room, and choosing a winnable path.

title: TBM 383: Maximizers vs. Focusers
questions: Why do maximizers and focusers keep misunderstanding each other at work?
How should leaders balance optionality and parallel bets with focus and coherence?
What does it mean to treat strategy as something other than a retrospective exercise?
How can organizations use multiple archetypes without letting their risks dominate?
summary: This post explains why leaders and teams get stuck in recurring conflicts between “maximizers” (speed, breadth, optionality) and “focusers” (constraints, coherence, depth). It argues that these differences are bias-driven rather than skill-driven, and proposes a framework for healthy organizations that preserve multiple archetypes while damping their failure modes. It’s aimed at leaders and strategists trying to improve execution and alignment when teams chase too much or do too little.

title: TBM 271: Diagnosing Your Company's Strategy Problem
questions: Why can’t our company converge on a strategy even when we have smart people?
Where is our insight gap—are we flying without radar or suffering from fabricated certainty?
Do we actually agree on the game and the options, or are we stuck in narrative clashes and factional risk profiles?
What prevents us from committing to a 'now what' when information is incomplete and every option has trade-offs?
search_terms: investor incentives vs founder incentives strategy
summary: This post offers a practical framework for leaders and operators to diagnose why a company seems strategically stuck. It argues that weak strategy often reflects failures across three pillars—information and insight coherence, shared clarity on the game/options, and realism about the 'now what' under incentives and incomplete knowledge—rather than a simple lack of strategic thinking. It is aimed at managers, directors, and executives who need to identify which gap is driving indecision and how to move toward focused commitments.

title: TBM 332: The Last Strategy Framework You'll Ever Need
questions: What questions should we answer first when building a strategy, and in what sequence?
How do we move from strategy frameworks as static artifacts to strategy as an interactive, team-latched process?
Why do strategy frameworks overlap yet still feel different, and how should we choose among them?
How do we make strategy decisions when we only have partial, low-confidence information?
search_terms: strategy kernel diagnosis guiding policy coherent actions
strategy narrative identity power inside organizations
summary: The post argues that strategy frameworks share a common underlying pattern (questions, tools, visualization, and deployment) and that the real difficulty of strategy comes from uncertainty and human organizational dynamics. It compares tool-heavy frameworks like Wardley Mapping and Zone to Win with decision-structure approaches like Rumelt’s strategy kernel, emphasizing how to choose and apply frameworks based on context. It’s aimed at product operations and teams trying to make strategy collaborative and actionable rather than a static, box-filling exercise.

title: TBM 331: Strategy & Decisiveness
questions: Is our problem truly strategy, or is it an inability to say no and prioritize?
How do we balance decisiveness with maintaining optionality and experimentation?
When should leadership intervene in trade-offs versus letting front-line teams navigate them?
How can we tell whether our strategy is revealing better choices or just justifying bad assumptions?
search_terms: strategy is not a magic elixir
how to prioritize when you can't do everything
summary: This post challenges the romantic idea that good strategy always looks bold and decisive, arguing that real strategy often mixes focus with optionality. It’s aimed at leaders and teams who feel stuck in multitasking, hedging, or unclear trade-offs, and want a practical way to decide when to intervene versus when to let front-line teams navigate. The core message is that strategy improves odds only when it acknowledges messy constraints and makes the right few decisions at the right time.

title: TBM 275: "Bad" Strategy. Why?
questions: Why does my company’s strategy not match the 'good strategy' examples from books and research?
Is what we call 'company strategy' actually the real strategy or just a negotiated narrative?
How can strategy be 'right' if different stakeholders want different outcomes?
What should I do when the strategy seems unwritten, implicit, or too inert to change?
search_terms: strategy inertia when things are working
summary: This post argues that many organizations lack a single, coherent 'company strategy' and instead operate through negotiated narratives and individual, often implicit strategies. It’s aimed at managers and practitioners who feel misaligned with their company’s stated strategy and want a practical way to interpret what’s really happening and how to act despite inertia.

title: TBM 229: Winnable and Unwinnable Games (Part 2)
questions: What are the central twists or balancing forces in my organization, and what job do they do?
Which actions technically follow the rules but violate the spirit of the game we should be playing?
When rules are repeatedly twisted, what new game gets created and what does it lead to?
How is the game in our company changing as we face disruption and new players?
search_terms: spirit of the game organizational culture
company as a game mechanics incentives
central twists crisis cards secret objectives
summary: This post uses board-game analogies to explain how organizations can be understood as “games” with different mechanics, balancing forces, and an underlying spirit that governs behavior beyond formal rules. It is aimed at leaders and culture/strategy practitioners who want practical mental models for diagnosing why cooperation or competition emerges, how rule-twisting creates new dynamics, and how companies can transition between games during disruption.

title: TBM 227: Explicit vs. Implicit Strategy
questions: How do you translate strategy into tangible commitments people can be held accountable to?
Why do organizations end up with implicit strategy that only shows up in OKRs and roadmaps?
How do you choose the most critical addressable challenges when prioritization is sensitive?
What practices help teams converge on a shared understanding of strategy?
search_terms: strategy translation to OKRs budgets forecasts
Rumelt crux coherent strategy challenges policies actions
why strategy doesn't become real until OKRs
safe space for hard strategy discussions
summary: The post contrasts explicit, coherent strategy with the implicit strategy that emerges when leaders avoid hard prioritization and instead communicate through goals, budgets, and commitments. It argues that most organizational members experience strategy as real only when it is deployed into day-to-day practices like OKRs, roadmaps, incentives, and constraints. It is aimed at leaders and product leaders trying to improve strategy convergence and alignment by creating time and psychological safety for difficult discussions.

title: TBM 221: Strategy = Insights^Conviction
questions: Does a strategy need to be unexpected to be effective?
How can you tell what a company’s strategy really is from the outside?
What uncomfortable NOs and guiding policies enable an “obvious” go-to-market move?
What unique twist, timing, or sequencing makes a standard play succeed when others fail?
search_terms: strategy is choosing what not to do
how to explain strategy with a twist
summary: The post argues that while simple, obvious strategies can be effective, the real substance of strategy lies in the guiding policies and “uncomfortable NOs” that determine what gets deprioritized, funded, measured, and risked. It’s aimed at product and go-to-market leaders who want a sharper way to diagnose and communicate why a standard play works for their company—through unique insights, capabilities, sequencing, and coherent actions.

title: TBM 218: Level, Depth, Time, and Frame
questions: How do I balance being high-level with being specific without losing strategic coherence?
How often should I update strategy templates and roadmaps across different time horizons?
What strategy “frame” should I use when communicating with different stakeholders?
How do I align quarterly plans, annual goals, and multi-year strategy so they reinforce each other?
search_terms: strategy dimensions level specificity time frame
Rumelt strategy diagnosis guiding principles coherent actions
summary: The post proposes a practical way to think about strategy by mapping it to four dimensions: level, depth (specificity), time horizon, and frame. It’s aimed at product managers and strategists who feel pulled between vague big-picture thinking and demands for concrete details, and it helps them choose the right strategy expression and update cadence. It also offers a mental model for how strategy artifacts and refresh cycles can coexist across multiple time horizons.

title: TBM 47/52: Prompts for Exploring Strategic Tensions and Polarities
questions: How can we use prompts to explore strategic tensions and polarities during annual planning?
Why do organizations avoid focus-inducing statements, and how does that affect planning?
What is a good facilitation approach for generating and refining strategy prompt answers?
How do we structure strategy decisions to account for counterintuitive tradeoffs and risky assumptions?
search_terms: focus as a threat political stalemate
turn the game into a game of
summary: The post provides strategy prompts designed to help teams navigate annual planning by explicitly surfacing strategic tensions and polarities. It argues that organizations often avoid such clarity due to internal politics, but that structured prompts can make planning more generative. It’s aimed at leaders and facilitators who want practical ways to run group exercises and refine strategy language without personalizing the exercise.

title: TBM 34/52: Words Matter—Strategy & Certainty
questions: How do I map my strategy’s underlying assumptions by certainty, specificity, and competitor consensus?
When should decreasing certainty trigger new or emergent strategic assumptions?
Why do probabilistic words create confusion when communicating strategy?
How can we communicate uncertainty coherently without manufacturing false certainty or waiting for 100% certainty?
search_terms: we believe highly unlikely probably not
summary: The post argues that strategy should be treated as a dynamic system of assumptions with varying certainty, direction, specificity, and asymmetric impact. It is aimed at product and strategy leaders who struggle to communicate uncertainty clearly, highlighting how probabilistic words are perceived differently by audiences. The guidance is to revisit assumptions and apply disciplined, word-by-word probabilistic language (or confidence intervals) to avoid strategy whiplash and false precision.

title: 33/52: "That Isn't Focus!"
questions: Why does a strategy that feels focused to me seem vague to someone else?
How much detail should a strategy include for a given audience and team experience level?
When using coarse-grained direction under uncertainty, how do we communicate certainty and next steps?
Does an open-ended strategy empower the team or signal leadership negligence?
summary: The post explains why “focus” in strategy can be perceived differently across people and teams, especially when experience, domain knowledge, and information needs vary. It offers guidance for communicating strategy more actionably by clarifying assumptions, the level of certainty, and how much detail is appropriate for the target audience. It is aimed at leaders and teams trying to align on direction and decision-making under uncertainty.

title: TBM 31/52: Why Does Our Strategy Keep Changing?
questions: Why does our strategy keep changing every six months?
Are we confusing tactics with strategy?
What biases or dynamics (like recency bias or premature convergence) are driving our yo-yo strategy?
Do we have a stable strategic core, or are we layering changes without a consistent diagnosis?
search_terms: elephant in the room strategy problem
summary: This post helps leaders diagnose why their organization’s strategy feels reactive and keeps changing, often creating “yo-yo” cycles and operational whiplash. It frames common failure modes—like tactics masquerading as strategy, recency bias, premature convergence, unresolved core issues, and watered-down strategy—and encourages teams to identify which root causes are present. It’s for executives and strategy teams seeking more stable strategic clarity and better decision-making around what to change and what to hold constant.

title: TBM 30/52: Why Don’t We Have a Strategy?
questions: Why do organizations reward reaction and output more than strategic thinking?
What’s missing when teams treat goals and pillars as “strategy”?
How do diagnosis, kernel, and guiding policies improve strategy resilience?
How does private strategy knowledge affect learning and plan revision?
search_terms: why we don't have a strategy
good strategy bad strategy coherent actions
productivity output rituals vs thinking time
summary: The post argues that the real barrier to strategy is not people’s inability to think, but organizational incentives and constraints that make strategic discourse and contemplation scarce. It uses Rumelt’s framework to distinguish strategy (diagnosis, kernel, guiding policies, and plausible immediate actions) from goal/pillar theater, and explains how keeping strategy private can trap learning in a small circle. It’s aimed at product and organizational leaders who want to build a culture where strategic thinking is practiced and shared.

title: TBM 28/52: First Focus. Then Simplify
questions: How do I tell the difference between focus and simplification when working on complex problems?
Why do teams make bad decisions when they approach complex challenges as simple ones?
What should a good strategy diagnosis do, and how is it different from making things “simple and memorable”?
When reducing many customer personas to a few, is it better to pick a subset or synthesize new personas?
search_terms: strategy diagnosis guiding policy coherent actions
summary: The post argues that complex problems should not be treated as simple, even if “simplifying” feels like reducing cognitive load. It’s aimed at leaders and teams who struggle to align on strategy, messaging, and customer personas, and need a practical way to diagnose first and then run focused experiments. It also highlights the common failure mode where teams confuse simple communication with true focus and context awareness.

title: TBM 27b/52: Why Most Strategies Lack Clarity
questions: Why do so many product strategies feel vague or incoherent?
Is it possible to have a clear strategy without being certain?
How should leaders communicate uncertainty to stakeholders who demand certainty?
What causes leaders to hedge or obfuscate strategy messaging?
search_terms: Miro mandate levels competitive analysis can should templates
summary: The post argues that many product strategies lack clarity because leaders equate coherence with certainty and then avoid uncertainty due to fear and stakeholder pressure. It’s aimed at product and strategy leaders who need a way to communicate a coherent direction while explicitly acknowledging what is known, assumed, and believed.

title: TBM 41/53: Strategy and Structure
questions: Is our strategy implicit or explicit, coherent, and plausible?
Does our organization design actually support the strategy, or are we just mapping teams to tasks?
When the environment shifts, can our strategy and org design keep up and adapt?
How can we restructure continuously and safely instead of relying on big-bang changes?
summary: This post offers a set of diagnostic questions for leaders and facilitators trying to understand why strategy changes don’t translate into real progress. It argues that failure often comes from treating strategy and structure as the same thing, leaving the old implicit strategy and org design in place while the environment shifts again. It’s aimed at teams navigating leadership transitions, strategy pivots, and the need for adaptive organizational design.
